description:
You are the system administrator of several Debian-based servers.
    For compliance reasons you must maintain a reproducible **package inventory**
    of a short, predefined list of core system utilities.

    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/sysadmin_logs  
       (create the parent directory if it does not yet exist).

    2. Inside that directory create a comma-separated values file named  
       /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.csv  

       2.1  The very first line **must be the header exactly as shown**  
            package,version  

       2.2  Collect the currently installed package version for each of the
            following three Debian packages *as reported by the local dpkg
            database* (do **not** hard-code any version; query the system):  

            • bash  
            • coreutils  
            • grep  

       2.3  Add one line per package to the CSV file using this precise format  
            (all lower-case, no extra spaces):  
            <package-name>,<version-string>  

            Example of a single (hypothetical) CSV line:  
            bash,5.1-6ubuntu1  

       2.4  Ensure the three data lines appear in **alphabetical order by
            package name** (bash comes before coreutils, etc.).

       When you finish, the file must therefore contain exactly **four**
       newline-terminated lines: one header line followed by three data lines.

    3. Still inside /home/user/sysadmin_logs create a second file named  
       /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.sha256  

       • Calculate the SHA-256 checksum of package_status.csv using any standard
         CLI tool (e.g. sha256sum).  
       • Write only the raw 64-character hexadecimal digest to the first line of
         package_status.sha256 (no filename, no spaces, no trailing text).  
       • The file must contain exactly that single line plus its terminating
         newline.

    4. When everything is done, display the contents of both files to the
       console so the maintainer can visually confirm the result.

    Formatting & verification requirements that **will be automatically
    tested**:

       • Directory /home/user/sysadmin_logs exists.  
       • /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.csv exists and contains
         exactly four lines.  
       • Line 1 is literally: package,version  
       • Lines 2-4 list the packages bash, coreutils, grep in that order and
         the version strings are exactly the versions dpkg currently reports
         (the test harness will verify this).  
       • /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.sha256 exists and contains
         exactly one 64-character hexadecimal digest followed by a newline.  
       • The SHA-256 digest recorded in package_status.sha256 matches the
         actual checksum of /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.csv.

    Important:  
    • You do *not* have root privileges; querying dpkg information is sufficient
      and does not require elevated permissions.  
    • Do **not** modify any system files. Only read package metadata and write
      to /home/user/sysadmin_logs.  
    • Follow the required output formats exactly so automated grading passes.
truth:
Expected artefacts the autograder will look for:

    1. Directory:
       /home/user/sysadmin_logs   (must exist, writable by normal user)

    2. Files and their required characteristics:

       a. /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.csv
          • 4 newline-terminated lines.
          • Line 1 literal text: "package,version"
          • Lines 2-4 (in this exact order):
              "bash,<VERSION_A>"
              "coreutils,<VERSION_B>"
              "grep,<VERSION_C>"
            where <VERSION_X> are the version strings returned by:
                dpkg-query -W -f='${Version}\n' <package>
          • File must be world-readable (mode 644 or similar).

       b. /home/user/sysadmin_logs/package_status.sha256
          • Single line, 65 bytes including the terminating newline.
          • Content must be the lowercase hexadecimal SHA-256 digest of
            package_status.csv with no filename or extra spaces.
          • File must be world-readable (mode 644 or similar).

    3. Integrity rule:
       SHA-256(package_status.csv) == the value inside
       package_status.sha256.  The autograder will recompute and compare.

    No other files are required; the presence, names and exact formats of the
    two files above will be used to determine success.
